text: A patient presented with continuous pelvic pressure for the last four to five months.
On examination, there was severe tenderness along the posterior fornix and the posterior vaginal wall.
We did a three D ultrasound following bowel preparation, but we were not able to locate any specific nodule.
The surgical field reveals deep endometriosis affecting the rectovaginal septum, with visible lesions and tissue involvement in this area.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting and lateralizing the ureter, followed by opening the peritoneum. The rectum is isolated on both lateral sides, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the adhesiolysis.
Since those steps have been shown in multiple previous videos, here we will start the main part of the video.
The surgeon proceeds with central dissection, focusing on separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The assistant uses a surgical instrument to grasp the rectum and applies cranial traction, elevating it within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes dense endometriotic adhesions without any visible nodules on the rectovaginal septum or Danone-Wheeler's fascia.
As you can see there,
The surgeon dissects the posterior vagina and rectal fat, opening these tissues further down towards the anorectal junction, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The anorectal junction is identified, revealing the serosal coat over the rectum densely adhered to the posterior vaginal wall, forming part of the Danone-Willers fascia.
The surgeon uses gentle traction and dissection with bipolar forceps and scissors to address the dense endometriotic adhesions, carefully separating the affected tissues.
This surgery can also be accomplished using other instruments like the laparoscopic shearer.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of bipolar forceps and scissors for dissection, focusing on separating dense adhesions between the posterior vaginal wall and the rectum.
The serosal coat is visibly adhered to the rectovaginal septum, showing dense plastering between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon opens the rectovaginal septum by moving away from the fibrotic planes, revealing the separation between the rectum and the posterior vaginal wall.

The surgeon continues the dissection in the caudal direction, approaching the levator ani muscle, which is located laterally to the anorectal junction, marking the endpoint of the dissection towards the caudal margin.
The trick of the dissection in deep endometriosis using bipolar and scissors is simple.
The surgeon applies gentle coagulation to the fibrotic tissue using a bipolar device, providing traction. Scissors are then used to make sharp cuts, further dissecting the fibrotic tissue.
One must be careful and always be aware of the bleeding which can happen due to the dense fibrosis in such patients.
The surgeon identifies fibrosis along the pararectal fascia, visible as dense fibrotic tissue adjacent to the rectal area.
The surgeon mobilizes the pararectal fascia, which is part of the rectal fascia, ensuring complete separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the caudal part of the rectal pillar, initially freeing it from surrounding tissue before applying coagulation to secure the area.
The surgeon completes the dissection, fully mobilizing the rectum away from the posterior fornix and the posterior vaginal wall, revealing the separation of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the longitudinal muscle layer and the distinct white appearance of the anorectal junction, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The next step is in order to prevent any recurrence from developing.
The surgeon dissects the anterior part of the rectal serosa, which forms the posterior part of the Denonvilliers fascia, removing it from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon demonstrates nerve-sparing techniques, preserving the rectal plexus from the inferior hypogastric nerve, with visible traction on the right side.
The surgeon begins to dissect and remove the Danone-Willers fascia, continuing the procedure to ensure complete separation from surrounding structures.

The surgeon makes gentle cuts over the serosal covering of the rectum, exposing the underlying longitudinal muscle.

The surgeon removes the fat layer above the exposed longitudinal muscle of the rectum, ensuring the lower one-third, approximately seven to eight centimeters, is completely bare by excising the entire Danone-Willers fascia.
Once the entire Danone-Willers fascia is taken off, the chances of having any recurrence in that plane is virtually negligible.
Following the surgery, the patient does not require to be given any post-operative depo preparations of GnRH analogues.
After completing the surgery, the surgeon inserts a rectal probe to assess and ensure the integrity of the rectum.

The surgeon fills the rectum with methylene blue, which is then removed to confirm its integrity. The lower part of the rectum is exposed, revealing the stripped longitudinal muscle coat visible for approximately seven to eight centimeters.
The entire nerve flexors are preserved and the patient usually passes bowel sounds within six to eight hours and gets discharged on the next day.
